mnemonics: COUNT THE ELEMENTS LIKE A CARDINAL COUNTS HIS FLOCK. Cardinality is the 'count' of elements in a set, even for infinite sets where we compare sizes using bijections (one-to-one pairings).
keyPoints: Cardinality is a measure of the 'number of elements' in a set.
Two sets have the same cardinality if there exists a bijective function between them.
A set is countably infinite if its elements can be listed in a sequence (e.g., natural numbers, integers, rationals).
The set of real numbers is uncountably infinite, proven by Cantor's diagonal argument.
The power set of any set has a strictly greater cardinality than the set itself.
commonMistakes: Assuming all infinite sets are the same size (e.g., thinking ℝ has the same cardinality as ℕ).
Confusing finite subsets of an infinite set with the infinite set itself (e.g., thinking the set of even numbers is smaller than ℕ; they have the same cardinality).
Misapplying the concept of cardinality to ordinary set operations without considering the necessary bijection.
